topicName: Hide and Seek - Views, Grids, and Maps
description: Video recommendation algorithm based on readiness assessment results for spatial reasoning: views of objects, grid positions, map reading, and spatial puzzles

excellent_performance.rationale: Student has excellent spatial awareness and direction sense, can fast-track to views and grids with challenges

videoSequence.reason: Introduction to top, side, and front views of objects
Different views of 3D shapes with examples
Positions on a grid using rows and columns
Reading simple maps and understanding symbols
Grid game and drone view exploration

ready_performance.rationale: Student is ready, provide complete coverage of views, grids, and maps

videoSequence.reason: Learn top, side, and front views of everyday objects
Explore views of 3D shapes in detail
Learn grid positions with rows and columns
Grid game and bird's eye view practice

partial_readiness.rationale: Student has gaps in directions or shapes, needs targeted review before main content

adaptiveLogic.description: Select prerequisite videos based on specific weak concepts

exampleSequence.reason: Review: Left, right, and basic directions
Start main topic: Top, side, and front views

not_ready.rationale: Student needs significant prerequisite review in directions, positions, and shapes before attempting views and grids

videoSequence.reason: Foundation: Learn left, right, and directions with a song
Essential: Learn above, below, beside, between
Critical: Recognize 3D shapes from different angles
Gentle intro to top, side, and front views
Begin main content: Grid positions basics

postPlaylistAction.message: After watching these review videos, try the readiness quiz again to check if you are ready to continue with views, grids, and maps.

conceptBasedRemediation.description: Map specific weak concepts to remedial videos

position_words.conceptName: Using position words (beside, between, in front of, behind)

comprehensionCheckpoints.description: Quick checks during video watching to adjust playlist

questions.question: If you look at a cone from the top, what shape do you see?
questions.explanation: A cone viewed from the top looks like a circle because the top of a cone is its circular base.

questions.question: A dice (cube) looks the same from the front, side, and top. What shape do you see each time?
questions.explanation: A cube looks like a square from every direction because all its faces are squares.

ifAllCorrect.message: Great job! You understand views of objects really well.

ifAllWrong.reason: Student struggling with understanding different views of shapes

questions.question: On a grid, to find a position at Row 2 and Column 3, you go:
questions.explanation: Row 2 means go down to the second row, and Column 3 means go across to the third column.

ifCorrect.message: Well done! You understand grid positions.

ifWrong.message: Practice finding positions on a grid before continuing to maps

skip_ahead.action: Skip to spatial reasoning puzzles for extra challenge

add_support.action: Pause main topic, insert prerequisite review on directions and shapes

suggest_break.trigger: After 4 videos watched in a single session
suggest_break.action: Suggest taking a 5-minute break before continuing

recommend_practice.trigger: Student completes videos but scores < 70% on checkpoint
recommend_practice.action: Recommend looking at real objects from different angles and drawing what you see

recommend_retake.trigger: Student in not_ready category completes all prerequisite videos
recommend_retake.action: Strongly recommend retaking readiness assessment before main content

high_score.message: Excellent! You know directions and shapes very well. You are ready to discover how objects look from different views and play grid games. We have created a fast-track playlist with some fun challenges!

good_score.message: Good work! You understand positions and shapes nicely. Let's explore views, grids, and maps with complete coverage of all topics.

partial_score.message: You have some gaps in directions or shape knowledge. Don't worry! We will start with targeted review videos on the concepts you need, then move on to views and grids.

low_score.message: You need to review directions, position words, and shapes first. We have created a playlist with fun videos to build a strong foundation. After watching, you can retake the quiz and then learn about views and maps.

conceptProgressionMap.description: Shows how spatial concepts build on each other

concepts: Shapes look different from different sides

playlistCompletion.description: % of students who watch >= 80% of recommended videos

practiceQuizPassRate.description: % of students who score >= 70% on practice quiz after watching playlist

videoRelevanceRating.description: Average student rating (out of 5) for playlist helpfulness

timeToMastery.description: Average minutes from readiness quiz to practice quiz pass

viewIdentification.description: % of students who can correctly identify top, side, and front views of common objects

gridNavigation.description: % of students who can locate positions on a grid using rows and columns

implementationNotes.recommendedValidation: Test with 15-20 Grade 4 students across all readiness levels

expectedOutcomes.excellent_students: Complete in 35-40 minutes with 90-100% practice quiz score
expectedOutcomes.ready_students: Complete in 35-40 minutes with 80-90% practice quiz score
expectedOutcomes.partial_students: Complete in 45-50 minutes with 70-80% practice quiz score (after prerequisite review)
expectedOutcomes.not_ready_students: Require prerequisite review (15 min) + retake + main content (25 min) = 45-55 total minutes, expected 60-75% on practice quiz

commonChallenges.challenge1: Students confusing top view with front view
commonChallenges.solution1: Use real objects like cups and books. Hold them above your head to see top view, hold in front to see front view
commonChallenges.challenge2: Students struggling with grid row/column coordinates
commonChallenges.solution2: Start with simple 3x3 grids and use color-coded rows and columns
commonChallenges.solution3: Start with maps of familiar places (classroom, school) before abstract maps
